Name: adaptive_rate_distortion_optimization
Title: Joint Rate-Distortion Optimization for Adaptive Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: By integrating an adaptive learning mechanism with rate-distortion theory, we can develop a model compression framework that dynamically adjusts its compression strategy based on task performance metrics, thus achieving improved predictive sufficiency and reduced distortion.
Related Work: Current research primarily addresses static compression strategies or uniform application across all layers of neural networks. Notably, the work by Sefidgaran et al. has established connections between generalization bounds and rate-distortion theory, but lacks a dynamic framework that adapts compression in real time. Our proposal stands out by incorporating adaptive mechanisms that respond to task-specific feedback, making it a novel extension rather than a trivial enhancement.
Abstract: In this proposal, we introduce a novel framework for model compression that synergizes adaptive learning techniques with rate-distortion theory. The core idea is to dynamically adjust the compression strategy of neural network layers based on real-time feedback from task performance metrics. By framing compression as a two-player game between a rate controller and a distortion evaluator, we aim to minimize task-relevant distortion while adhering to bit-rate constraints. Our approach will employ mutual information and entropy measures to quantify the trade-offs involved, leading to an operational bound on bits-per-weight that preserves predictive sufficiency. We will validate our framework through empirical experiments across various architectures and datasets, measuring improvements against established benchmarks in both lossless and lossy compression contexts. The expected outcomes include a deeper understanding of the trade-offs in adaptive compression and concrete bounds on achievable rates.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model compression strategy using fixed quantization and pruning techniques to establish benchmarks.

2. Develop an adaptive model that utilizes feedback from task performance to adjust compression ratios dynamically, employing a reinforcement learning approach to optimize the rate-distortion trade-off.
3. Evaluate the models on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and measure predictive performance using metrics like accuracy, F1 score, and bit-rate efficiency.
4. Conduct ablation studies to assess the impact of different adaptive strategies on compression effectiveness and task performance.
5. Analyze the results to derive the operational bounds on achievable rates and the distortion-rate curves.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. Complexity in implementing adaptive mechanisms may lead to challenges in stability and convergence during training.
2. The performance gains could be marginal if the underlying task does not significantly benefit from adaptive compression.
3. The computational overhead of real-time adjustments might offset the benefits of compression in resource-constrained environments.
